tweets.text:
This chart provides some good context for where we are today. You can see a few anomalies on the chart. 

Two examples include 2018 and 2020. In 2018, failed expectations of material inventory draws in Q4 2018 led to the largest long liquidation in money manager positioning in history.

In 2020, massive demand destruction led to a supply glut that quickly translated to much lower oil prices down the road.

Today, the market is essentially pricing in builds to reverse from 1.05 billion bbls (expected) back to 1.2 billion bbls in the US.

tweets.text:
I have no idea how true it is that US SPR operational minimum is closer to ~300 million bbls, but at the current trajectory, we will reach ~680 million bbls by the end of July (380 million bbls for US commercial crude and 300 million bbls for SPR). 

US crude exports have to shut off, so please expect a major drop in US crude exports after July.

Note: Line past the dot is the estimate for next week's report.
tweets.full_text:

US crude exports have to shut off, so please expect a major drop in US crude exports after July.

Note: Line past the dot is the estimate for next week's report.

tweets.text:
This is my analysis of the Strait of Hormuz and you can see it in the video from @MarineTraffic. 

Tanker inflows remain heavily restricted. I have included a table of June 24 data thus far.

What's obvious to me is that there's an exodus of tankers using the Oman route. You can see this at the beginning of the video and towards the end of the video. There's another group ready to leave.

The US appears to be coordinating with these groups to support transit. 

Again, most of the traffic is outbound as they are trying to get stranded tankers out.

IRGC just announced that tankers must adhere to the rules. But so far, there's not much enforcement on the Oman lane. The longer the transits continue on the Oman lane, the faster IRGC will lose control of the Strait. This means that escalation is a high probability scenario.

Price still goes down.

tweets.full_text:
Rollbit had DAILY buybacks and a token-incentivized structure.

Price still goes down.

PumpFun, Aster, etc. buybacks, sometimes in the millions. Some “big” announcements in the pipeline.

Price still goes down.

Sooner you realize that this mirage is a feature and not a bug, you’ll understand why there’s a disconnect between expectations and reality.
